notes.anchor: I gave my Cicero his white toga at Arpinum
notes.note: Cicero performs the toga virilis ceremony, marking his son Marcus's passage to manhood, at the family seat of Arpinum because Rome was barred to them; the rite was normally a public Roman occasion, so its displacement underscores the dislocation of civil war.

notes.anchor: in a civil and unholy war
notes.note: Bellum civile, war between fellow citizens, was felt by Romans as a peculiarly polluting evil; calling it "unholy" (nefarium) marks it as an offence against the gods as well as the state. Cicero's recoil from it shapes his whole anguished neutrality.

notes.anchor: he who sent his son to crush Cnaeus Pompeius
notes.note: The eminent jurist Servius Sulpicius Rufus (cos. 51 BC) had let his son join Caesar's army besieging Pompey at Brundisium; Cicero, who esteemed Servius's caution, judges him the most pitiless of the trimmers for so abandoning Pompey's cause.

notes.anchor: since the upper sea is blockaded
notes.note: The "upper sea" is the Adriatic (mare Superum), the "lower" the Tyrrhenian (mare Inferum); with Caesar's forces controlling the Adriatic crossing to Pompey in Greece, Cicero plans a roundabout escape down the western Italian coast.

notes.anchor: we shall make for Croton, or for Thurii
notes.note: Croton and Thurii were ports on the instep and toe of Italy in the far south; from there Cicero hoped to round the peninsula and sail east, eventually toward Egypt, to reach Pompey by the long way about.
